     Thus
         the last rows at the edge of the board for white consists of fields from e9 to i9 followed by i9 to i5, while
         the last rows at the edge of the board for red consists of fields from a5 to a1 followed by a1 to e1.
    


				
				
					Capturing
     Men can capture an adjacent opponent's piece (man or king) by jumping over it following a straight line onto the empty field directly behind the opponent's piece.
     Capturing is compulsory, including making chained jumps if available. Thus men's turn may consist in chained jumps. 
     If more than one capture move is available in a player's turn then it is obligatory to select the capturing move resulting in capturing the maximum amount of the opponent's pieces (men or kings).
     If optional chained capturing might capture same amount, the player may choose.
     Men are non flying. Thus they can only capture an opponent's piece adjacent to their own position. 
     Kings are flying. They may move over any amount of empty fields or capture a piece in any amount of empty fields' distance away as long as the adjacent field behind the captured piece is empty. Anyway if a king captures, it may land on any empty field immediately behind or following empty fields in straight line of the captured piece unless it is required to stop on a specific field in order to continue jumping according to the rule of capturing maximum amount of opponent's pieces.
     Any capture move may be performed by men or kings in any direction - forwards or backwards.
     Each jump is performed in a straight line over any single opponent's piece. The adjacent field behind the pieces captured must be free.
     A possible chained jump can consist of single jumps having a combination of same or even different directions.
     In a chained capture move opponent's pieces are removed only after capturing is complete.
     During a chained capture a piece may not be jumped more than once. But it is allowed to revisit or jump over any empty field multiple times then.
     There is no breezing as a penalty for captures that are not performed.

Mach Speed Hunters 
Aerial Combat in the Modern Age
35 page PDF

Mach Speed Hunters is a complete miniatures game of air combat in the missile-armed Modern era. The basic rules allow for relatively large battles with up to a dozen aircraft per side and minimal record keeping.

Optional rules are included which cover; air to ground combat, bombers, air-to-ground munitions, variable pilot quality and experience, and simple campaign system.

Advanced rules which add realism include; advanced maneuvering, deflection shooting, advanced air-to-air missiles, countermeasures, visual spotting, limited ammunition, critical damage, and more...

Also included is a missile reference and the Afterburners aircraft lists, with game stats for 118 different aircraft from 12 different nations.

Download version can be purchased at www.wargamedownloads.com

Knights in the Sky
Aerial Combat in the First World War
31 page PDF

Knights in the Sky is a complete miniatures game of WWI air combat. The basic rules allow for relatively large battles with up to a dozen aircraft per side and minimal record keeping.

Optional rules are included which cover; air to ground combat, bombers, observation balloons, Zeppelins, variable pilot quality and experience, and simple campaign system.

Advanced rules which add realism include; advanced maneuvering, deflection shooting, visual spotting, limited ammunition, critical damage, and more...

Also included is the Canvas Falcons aircraft lists, with game stats for 99 different aircraft from 6 different nations.

Download version can be purchased at www.wargamedownloads.com

MiG Hunters
Aerial Combat in the 1950's
25 page PDF

MiG Hunters is a complete miniatures game of air combat in the transitional era of the 1950's. The basic rules allow for relatively large battles with up to a dozen aircraft per side and minimal record keeping.

Optional rules are included which cover; air to ground combat, bombers, variable pilot quality and experience, and simple campaign system.

Advanced rules which add realism include; advanced maneuvering, deflection shooting, visual spotting, limited ammunition, critical damage, and more...

Also included is the Swept Wings aircraft lists, with game stats for 38 different aircraft from 4 different nations.

Download version can be purchased at www.wargamedownloads.com

Where is the steam engine headed? The numbers on the backs of the station cards indicate the distance. Whoever pays attention and has a good memory will turn over the right cards and move the Little Steam Engine a long way. Little lumps of coal are given out as a reward.

Subtitled "Viking Age Skirmish Rules," Strandhogg (translation: "Beach Raid") is a set of miniature wargame rules for 25/28mm or 15mm games simulating Viking raids and small battles of the era of the Sagas.

The rules cover Leadership with several categories including Nobles, Jarls, Ealdormen and Lords; Commanders (Huscarls, Thanes and Milites) and troop types including Warriors, Levy, Archers/Slingers/Crossbowmen, Berserkers, and Armed Peasants/Townfolk.

Warbands are composed of various combinations of these types up to an agreed-upon point value; different categories of soldier have differing values, and accessories such as Spears and Shields add to a type's cost.  A Record Sheet is made out with values for each Leader and Warband involved in a game.

Initiative is determine by use of cards from a conventional card deck.  Each player is assigned a card number or suit, adding a card for each Warband under a player's control.  Additional cards can be added to reflect additional events or participants in a given scenario, for example, the random appearance of a traveling Lord or Bishop and retinue.

Dynamics of play involve Morale, Courage, Agility, Terrain, Non-Warband Movement, Shieldwalls and Wedges, the use of ships, and Mounted forces.  Information is provided for Saxons and Normans as well as Vikings, and the system is also adaptable to other peoples with whom the Vikings interacted, including Picts, Arabs or Skraelings (Native Americans) with a little historical research on the gamer's part.

The game is well suited to multiplayer games, each player handling one or more Warbands of anywhere from six to twenty or more figures on the table.  Game length is dependent upon the scenario design, from swift ship-to-ship fights to extended coastal raids or open-field battles.

The game is enhanced by the inclusion of a random events card deck which adds variety and surprises to play.

Unlike many sets of miniatures rules, Strandhogg can be enjoyed by players owning only a few dozen figures; an average Warband might number ten to twelve figures, and three Warbands compose a raiding force under a Noble.  The rulebook is sprinkled with sidebars featuring excerpts from the Norse Sagas for flavor, and also includes optional rules for special attributes or traits to individualize Leaders or leading warriors (for example, a warrior surnamed "the Stout" gains +1 on Defense; "Leg Biter" enjoys a +1 on Offense).

Players will often find themselves identifying strongly with the fortunes of their warband and even individuals within it; although Strandhogg is not a role-playing game, it deliberately strives to impart a strong flavor of its Viking focus.

Web address: STRANDHOGG@rochester.rr.com

The goal of The Beautiful Game is to collect a full side of 11 players and make it to the World Cup final.

As you progress in the game, your team will change.  Players may be booked on a red or yellow card.  Poor players may be sent to the bench until they improve.  Injuries?  You betcha!

There are many ways to gain or lose players.  Answering World Cup trivia, solving puzzles, and performing tasks are just some of the things you'll find yourself doing to improve your team.
Snap Bowling is a huge game by Ideal. The "alley" measures 40 inches long by 13 inches wide. The 10 pins have magnets in the bottom for easy setup.

(from back of box)

Scooby-Doo and his friends are all dressed up in this fun and exciting guessing game. Each player gets their own game console with 24 different pictures on it. Secretly choose one of the characters - Chef Scooby-Doo, Baseball Daphne, Sombrero Velma, Bearded Shaggy, Fred with Glasses or any one of the other characters. On your turn, ask the other player a "Yes" or "No" question as you try to guess who they chose before they guess your card. As you get answers to your questions you'll start to figure out who your opponent picked. The first player to make a correct guess wins.

The original 1965 release comes in a very large 19" x 18" x 8" box.  It features a plastic scarecrow (which is almost as large as the box) with various scoring targets - hat (5 points), crows on arms (10 or 15 points) and a 50 point target on the scarecrow's coat which, when hit, causes the scarecrow's coat to fall off in pieces.  A dart gun with three suction-cup darts is also included.

A turn consists of firing all three darts at the scarecrow from a pre-agreed distance. If a player causes the coat of the scarecrow to fall off, 50 points is scored and their turn ends, regardless of the number of darts in reserve.

The first player to accumulate 300 points is the winner.

Game was re-released in 1975 in a very slightly different box.

The Open Sesame Game is a game for up to four players. Spin the spinner and put 1, 2, or 3 jewels into the mountain. If the spinner lands on the Open Sesame space then each player quickly puts their marble into the mouth of the volcano. The first marble down the hole will open the door and spill the jewels.

The Four Over Game claims to take the skill of pinball and the logic of checkers. Flip the steel ball to find out how many moves, then move your checkers across the board to the other player's home. The ball may knock some checkers out as it falls into the numbered slot.

The object of Hokie Golf is the same a regular golf: end the game with the lowest score.  Everyone gets an initial hand of four cards, two of which are unknown.  Players then take turns drawing cards, discarding unwanted cards from their hand.  If you think you have the lowest score, you can pass your turn, ending the hole once everyone else gets one last draw.  Lowest score wins the hold.  Play nine holes for a short game, or eighteen for a full game.

Contains 111 Cards and Scoresheets.

Postcard expansion to base game California by Michael Schacht published in 2006.


     Number 2 in the Abacusspiele / Spiele aus Timbuktu postcard-series


Also part of the expansion NEUE BONUSKARTEN.

Object of this game is to deliver 2 loads to the 2 oases and return to the staring point first.

The loads are carried by nice camels or dromedaries and movement depends on the number of loads as well as on the wind, which is determined with a roll of a spinner. The spinner is also responsible for determining how the sand dunes are shifting and if there are some Fata Morganas on the horizon.
 ( Fata Morganas are caused by hot and cold air which distorts the appearance of objects on the horizon either at sea or in the desert to make them look like, well, castles in the air.)


Additionally, there are some fixed desert fields on the board, but the main problem is, that when encountering such obstacles you need the use of water canisters. Each player has only 6 canisters and losing all of them means a restart or sometimes an elimination of the game.

The deck has eight suits and 13 cards for each suit.
Players get ten cards at random and use them to bid (simultaneously) on a card revealed from the deck: the highest bid chooses if that card must be discarded (and everyone draws a card) or kept on the table. Then another card is revealed and bid upon. When five cards are on the table, each player scores accordingly to the five cards he still has in hand (due to matching suits and values with the cards on the table). 
The rulebook presents three scoring systems (one must be selected beforehand) to offer different levels of complexity.

The theme of this game is smuggling cigars, wine, rum, watches, cigarettes and money between Germany, Austria and Switzerland on skis! Thus, an unusual theme for the alps...

There are 3 home bases, where the players can buy up to 3 smuggling goods and start their way to deliver them to another base. Movements are not done by a dice roll, but the more goods a player is carrying, the slower will be his speed. this can be dangerous, because their is a fast moving frontier-guard, who will confiscate the goods, when he catches a player.

Before starting, the players should know, which smuggling goods give the best profit - watches are cheap to buy in Switzerland and give the most in Germany for example.

The game has double sided playing money, which comes in the (old) currencies of the 3 countries: Deutsche Mark, Schilling and Schweizer Franken.

This is a sports car racing game with a variable board of 6 pieces and 4 movable chicanes.

Each player gets a board, where he indicates the speed, the additional gasoline and the brake reserves. The later two things are needed, when a player wants to reduce or amount his speed by more than 1 click on a 1-8 table.

There are also 2 small decks of cards, which are used for pit stops or when a car goes off the track. Some of the cards also influence the condition of the track, so racing gets even harder to master than it is anyway.

The game belongs to a series of 4 square cardboard games from Scandecor Play Racing, Play Olympic, Play Western, Play Pirates. It has funny comic drawings, which could easily be from Jean Jacques Loup, who has made many illustrations for puzzles.

This is a rather simple dice game, where 2-6 players play up to 17 sport events. It belongs to a series of 4 square cardboard games from Scandecor (Play Racing, Play Olympic, Play Western, Play Pirates).

All events are done with the roll of 1-3 dice and give 7-5-4-3-2-1 points for rank 1-7.

As with all games of the series, the board is illustrated with funny comics and the 6 plastic playing pieces are also made in that funny design.

Another game of the 4 square cardboard games from Scandecor (Play Racing, Play Olympic, Play Western, Play Pirates).

A railroad track crosses the longish board and a small train drives from left to right (and back if a longer play is wanted) with gold barrels in his wagon.

Each player has 2 desperadoes who must try to steal these gold barrels and bring them in his hideout. A turn consists of a roll of 2 dice, where one must be used for the movement of the train. Since the train must stop at all 5 stations spread around the board, it moves slow enough, that desperadoes of several players will meet it quite often and they can try to kill each other and take the gold barrel in this way.

As with all games of the series, the board is illustrated with funny comics.

Another game of the 4 square cardboard games from Scandecor (Play Racing, Play Olympic, Play Western, Play Pirates).

Before the game starts, several land pieces must be laid on the large gameboard, so you'll get a board with some islands and some fortified harbors.

Each player choses one home harbor, where his 4 ships start. Movement is done by dice roll, but it can be modified by some of the included cards.

12 of these cards indicate, where a treasure is located.   
The game ends, when all 12 treasures are found.

As with all games of the series, the board is illustrated with funny comics.

This game belongs to a series of 4 games by Scandecor, who come in a square plastic box (Play Cavalry, Play Balance, Play Ecology and Play Numeric).

Play Ecology looks like a mixture of "Trap the Cap" and "Chess", but it is not as easy as that sounds. Each of the players has 6 playing pieces, which move like the Knights in chess. In the middle of the board is one neutral piece, who carries a wooden ball on his crater shaped top.

Object of the game is to catch that wooden ball (the playing pieces all have small craters on their top to put the ball on top) and bring it to your edge of the 9x9 game board. The neutral piece moves like the king in chess and it can make captures by jumping on other pieces - like in "Trap the Cap". What makes the game interesting is, that the neutral piece is the only piece that can capture normal pieces. That means, that you need at least 1 piece to capture the ball, but the other pieces can only try to block other pieces from doing so.

This game belongs to a series of 4 games by Scandecor, which come in a square plastic board (Play Cavalry, Play Balance, Play Ecology and Play Numeric).

The board has 12x12 spaces and each of them is made as a 3d-hole, where you can place a tile looking to one player or to the other by tip over the tile.

One player plays with the 48 water tiles and the other takes the 48 land tiles. Each tile can be moved 1 space in advance. Captures (accomplished sideways) can be done when a tile can move to an empty space, which has an opponents tile directly adjacent to it. Since the tiles are facing in 2 directions, the rules use the term "overshadowed" for such tiles. The capture is done by tipping over the captured piece and if there are additional tiles directly behind the captured piece, you can tip over these tiles also.

The game ends, when all positions are blocked, that is when no reasonable moves can be made by neither player. You simply count the tiles then to see, if the water or land player has won the game.

This game belongs to a series of 4 games by Scandecor, which come in a square plastic board (Play Cavalry, Play Balance, Play Ecology and Play Numeric). )

The game is played on a 12x12 board. The game can be played with 2 or 4 players. Half of the players take the industrial pieces, the others the nature pieces. When 2 players play, each has the following 47 pieces: 
- 18 roads/rivers, 12 straight and 6 curved
- 24 houses/trees
-  4 factories/volcanoes
-  1 pollutions/earthquakes (disasters)

If playing with 4, each player gets half the pieces and the 2 additional pollution/earthquake tiles are used also.

The game begins with an empty board except the 2 or 4 disaster tiles, which are placed in the corners of the board. The players then start to place roads and rivers on the board and as soon as the first road/river is placed, they can also start to lay down the other pieces. Houses and factories can be placed 1-2 spaces besides the road, trees and volcanoes 1-2 spaces besides the river.

Factories/volcanoes protect the surrounding spaces from being overbuilt by the opponents. These restrictions make it more and more difficult to place the tiles on the board. Winner is the player/party who can lay down all their pieces.

Object of the game is to be the first, who can play all of their pieces.

This game belongs to a series of 4 games by Scandecor, which come in a square plastic board (Play Cavalry, Play Balance, Play Ecology and Play Numeric).

This game is played on a 12x12 board and each players has 9 dice as playing pieces. These dice look like normal 6-sided dice, but 2 sides of them are free. The are 2 types of dice, odd and even dice. Odd dice show 1-3-3-5 on their sides, even dice 2-4-4-6.

Object of the game is to win the most points, which are given for capturing opposing dice. You can also win points, when you succeed in bringing an own dice to the opposite corner of the board.

At the start, the 9 dice per player start in one corner of the board and they show 1,2,2,3,3,3,4,4,5 on top. When a die moves to an adjacent empty space, its value is not changed, but a die can also be used to jump over another, if the value on top is within a range of 1 to the jumping die. Then a die can be turned to change its initial value.

The game is rather slow developing, because it takes some time, until the dice approach each other on the large 12x12 board.

Zehnkampf (=Decathlon) is a dice game with very short rules. All 10 Decathlon events are decided by simple dice rolls, but the rules for the running events are a little more confusing and require some work in order to understand them.

The results of the 10 events are scored just like a real Decathlon. The result of this is, that some events have bigger point ranges than others, so it would be better to make an extra effort in these events... but since it's a simple dice game, there is no room to make extra efforts.

The game is played on a hex board, which has 97 holes. The board is placed upright on a plastic base and all 97 holes must be filled with 97 pegs. The pegs have to be placed so that both ends overhang the same length on both sides of the board.

Each player sits on one side of the vertical board and before starting, each must secretly hang his red playing piece on one of the 97 pegs.

Each turn, a player now takes off 1 peg off the board and he is free to turn the board by 45 degrees before taking a peg off.

The 2 playing pieces are formed like a horseshoe and if the opponent takes off a peg with a horseshoe hanging on it, it will fall down to the next peg - or on the table, if none is below. Winner is the player who succeeds in letting the opposing horseshoe fall on the table.

This game has a 16x16 game board, but the playing pieces move on the 15x15 intersections.

The second and third row on each side of the board are the player's home sections, the space between is neutral. Each player has 6 "normal" playing pieces (1 tesseract, 1 cylinder, 1 pyramid, 1 rectangle, 2 stars), 2 large slide bars, 5 small slide bars and 2 crosses.

At the start, each player places the Tesseract, the pyramid, one star and 2 small slide bars in his section. Then the 2 crosses are placed in the neutral zone. These crosses cannot be moved and remain at their position until the end of the game. The remaining pieces: the cylinder, the rectangle, one star and 5 slide bars form the reserves.

Each turn, players now move their pieces over the board or place a piece from their reserves everywhere on the board. Object of the game is to either capture the opposing tesseract or capture all other "normal" pieces of the opponent.

The movements of the playing pieces different for every type. Interesting, that slide bars can capture normal pieces, but cannot be captured themselves.

The game is played within the box itself. The bottom has 30 slots to keep the available shares and 4 trays to save the money of the bank. The top cover can be left open and it indicates the current value and dividends for each title.

The game includes 5 shares numbered 1-5 in 6 different colors.

Players throw one 6-sided die and one color-die to determine which of the shares can be bought, pay dividends or maybe give special gratifications. If a 6 is thrown, the share of the thrown color is given a new value by an additional dice throw.

The game starts with 200.000 for each player and the one who collects  1 million first is the winner

In this games players are maneuvering their fishing ship on a  mobile board circular which emulate currents and obstacle. The object of the game is to catch and sell a variety of fish of different market value.

Clearly positioned as a family game (and designed by the members of a single family), this French micro-edition is also an educational game trying to provide some insights about sea and the coastal fishing industry.

An interesting economic game, where players try to become the first player, who could buy and purchase 15-18 goods of 6 different types.

Several taxes and fees must be paid on the way to becoming the winner of the game. A few of them are fixed amounts, which must be paid when a player reaches a certain step on the way up, some must be paid, when a player misses a chance to buy or purchase goods and therefore has to draw 1 of 80 event cards.

There are six different goods available. A player can purchase 1-3 goods of a chosen type depending on a dice throw. Red goods can only be purchased by throwing a 6, green goods need at least a 5, yellow ones a 4 and so on.
All goods cost 10.000 per piece, but the selling price is higher for the harder goods, from 16.000 - 32.000.

Before selling, another dice throw is required, which needs matching  the throws while purchasing the goods.
 Last but not least, the purchase and sale of goods can be challenged by other players, if their  bid  was either lower or higher than the agreed on transaction.

boardgame: Benny Goodman Swings into a Game of Musical Information
description:
"Benny Goodman Swings into a Game of Musical Information" is a Benny-Goodman-endorsed game which includes an 8-note metal xylophone with a wooden mallet, which is used for a music-guessing portion of the game, similar to Name That Tune.

One player acts as a "Music Professor" (a sort of host for the game), while the other players are his students.  The students are quizzed on their knowledge of popular songs and classical music.  Challenges include straight question-and-answer trivia, guessing songs from their chorus, and guessing songs which the professor plays on the xylophone.  The player with the highest score, based on number of correct answers, is the winner.

"The Monkees Game", also known as "Hey Hey The Monkees Game", was a licensed game from Transogram (makers of many other 1960s licensed games) based on the popular NBC musical comedy series.

Players used pawns based on one of the four Monkees - Peter Tork, Davy Jones, Mike Nesmith, or Mickey Dolenz.  The board is designed to look like sheet music.  The element of chance is added by a spinner.  One unusual feature in some editions is a colorful six-note xylophone, with which players can make the Monkees theme tune, for which sheet music is included. Another edition includes a "four string" (two rubber band) plastic guitar along with the sheet music insert.

The box art is an illustration of the Monkees riding in a red GTO hot-rod convertible.

Based on the game show of the same name hosted by Gene Rayburn, which ran on NBC from 1958-60.

The game was played similarly to Name That Tune, but with only the first three notes of a popular song being played, and players then bid on their ability to guess the song correctly. The game cost more than the typical board game for the era due to including a small, eight-note xylophone which players used to begin each song.

How's this supposed to work again? What happens when a team of two explainers are supposed to describe a concept but can only say the sentences together? But it gets better: the players can't discuss what they want to say ahead of time and must build the sentence while alternating words! A good time is guaranteed because the rest all try to figure out what the concept is at the same time. Who will have the most points?

This set of Team Work "Fußball 1" covers the topic soccer.

Expands:

    Team Work Original

How's this supposed to work again? What happens when a team of two explainers are supposed to describe a concept but can only say the sentences together? But it gets better: the players can't discuss what they want to say ahead of time and must build the sentence while alternating words! A good time is guaranteed because the rest all try to figure out what the concept is at the same time. Who will have the most points?

This set of Team Work "Fußball 2" covers the topic soccer.

    Team Work Original

boardgame: Asterix & Obelix: Die Lorbeeren des Caesar
description:
'Explore Europe' (aka Europareise} meets 'Malefiz' - two (almost) classic German games combined to form an Asterix travel game across Europe. 
Players have to visit 5 locations before they can pick up Caesar’s laurels in Rome. Before they can deliver them to their village they have to visit another 5 locations. Blocking pieces simulate the 'evil Romans'.

Object: to be the first player to successfully reach all three EarthAlert Recycle Centers and earn a Unit of Oxygen at the final Recycle Center.

There are three types of cards needed to move around the board:

Question and Answer, in which a player must answer a true/false or multiple choice environmental question.

Definitions, in which player must define a geographical or environmental word or location.

Roleplay, in which player must guess what subject is being acted out by two other players. Example: One player must play the role of a pregnant woman, the other player is a taxi cab. The answer is "birth."

Each time a recycle center is entered, a recycle card is drawn with hints and suggestions on how to save the environment.

To answer any card incorrectly is to lose a Unit of Oxygen. But in the end, there is always the "Last Gasp" opportunity.

2-6 players start with 18.500 gaming money and when the first player goes bankrupt, the game ends and the player with the most money wins the game.

Included in this game is 1 color-die and 6 dice in the colors shown on the color-die. There is also a paying board, which shows numbers from 4-20 and the corresponding payout amount.

Each turn consists of several dice throws, done by the "active" player. The inactive players can place fix bets (of 100) before each dice throw and all winning amounts are to be paid by the active player!

The dice throws are done by using the following procedure: the dice with the color showing on the color-die is placed on the paying board and it's number is added to the numbers of the dice already there.

Before the active player goes on throwing the remaining dice, the other players can retire and let them give the won amount from the active player. Fortunately, the active has also a winning option: when the color-die shows a color of a die already on the paying board, his turn end immediately and he collects all bets from the other players.

This is a horse racing game, where placing bets on the results is more important than the races themselves.

10 races are run with the help of 22 playing cards and each race has a fixed number of possible bets for a total of 21 bets. The amounts for these bets are also predefined, so that a player has exactly 21 different bet amounts for the whole game.

All bets must be placed before a race, so you depend heavily on luck, if you draw the best cards for your bets.

The races are funny to play. The cards have 2 colors on each side and cards must always match the color of the preceding one. One of the 22 cards marks the finishing line and the next 3 cards after that line give the running-in.

From the packaging text:

"Twist & Hold 'Em Poker Cube features an entire deck of cards on one cube. Your 'hand' is the side that's facing you and the cards are 'shuffled' by turning the rows cube-style. Take anywhere for fun for the whole family!"

Pressman #1493 is basically a Rubik-style cube with stickers having playing card denominations on the faces. Also included are two six-sided dice. One of the dice lists a series of rotation instructions, and the other is the usual pip layout. Both dice have faces in six different colors that correspond to the centers of each face on the main card cube. The values of the central cube spaces increase by twos from 2 to Q (12) and progress through all four suits (hearts & spades are represented twice). The edge pieces are always two cards with a numerical difference of two and are always different in color. Similarly, the corner pieces progress in increments of two, half of them clockwise and half counterclockwise, and always two black and one red or two red and one black, with like colors never in matching suits. Rules for playing variants of Texas Hold 'Em, Black Jack, Vegas, Spades, Chicago Hi/Lo and Rummy are provided, but others could probably be adapted.

A two player derivative of the Morris family of games, which can be played with partners.  The object of the game is to get three pegs in a row on either the solid or dotted lines.  Each time you do so, you get to remove an opponents piece.  The game ends when either you opponent concedes or has only two pegs left.

boardgame: Tooty Frooty: the New Mr. Potato Head Game
description: Mr. Potato Head played Cootie style.  Each player selects a plastic head (potato, cucumber, orange or carrot), then spins the large spinner to determine what (if any) piece is added to their character.

The "New All-American Party Thriller!" from 1942...Victo is a WWII era Bingo game with quotes from American war heroes and leaders replacing the usual I-24.  Players are reminded on every card to "Buy U.S. War Bonds & Stamps."

Mid-sixties Marvin Glass offering from Schaper for two players only.

The modular 'board' consists of a high quality hollow green plastic beanstalk about 18" high with a fluffy white cloud surrounding the top, concealing the 'sleeping' giant.  A sequence of ten small holes spirals from bottom to top of the beanstalk on each side.  Each of the holes is one of three shapes and these accommodate the stems of the plastic leaves players must insert in the correct sequence from bottom to top.

To start the game, all beanstalk leaves are placed inside the hollow beanstalk through a slot situated on each side.  There is a sliding apparatus at the base of the beanstalk which allows each player access to a single leaf on their turn.

The plastic beanstalk leaves come in 3 colors - green, gold & black.  The green leaves have stems that will only fit into specific beanstalk holes and must be replaced inside the beanstalk if they do not match the next in the sequence.  Gold leaves are universal and fit into any beanstalk hole.  Black leaves are useless and are immediately replaced through a slot.

The first player to complete the sequence of leaves in order wins the game.  When a leaf is placed in the final hole at the top of the beanstalk, a spring mechanism is triggered which causes the 'sleeping' giant to pop up.
family: Folk Tales & Fairy Tales: Jack and the Beanstalk

description:
Season Harvest is a resource-gathering game played on a modular board made of cards.  There are four card types: summer, fall, winter, and spring.  As you play cards to change the seasons in spaces on the board, seeds grow into trees which eventually bear fruit.  Your goal is to harvest as much fruit as possible and end the game with the most fruit in your basket.

Although the basic game is competitive, Season Harvest also has several cooperative variants.

http://www.invisible-city.com/play/292/season-harvest

Minnesota Minutiae is a boxed deck of trivia cards, featuring the state of Minnesota.  Categories include music, politics, sports, geography and history.  No instructions or score-keeping bits are included, the cards may be designed as a replacement set for an existing trivia game.

From the website:

TOTAL PLAY FOOTBALL™ is a card game designed for non-stop action. Players able to recognize different Offensive card plays and make Defensive counter plays will certainly have the advantage. There is no turn for play in the Total Play Football Card Game. Any player can make a play for points or deny points at any time which adds even more fun and excitement to the game. For the love of the game called football and the advent of new technology, football can be played a myriad of ways. No one will feel excluded from playing Total Play Football™ because the game terminology does not require football lingo. All you need is the desire to win at an appealing and entertaining card game that is loads of fun for the whole family. The only way for you to know how well you will embrace the Total Play Football™ card game is to play it.

From the website:

4th Quarter Touchdown Football challenges your ability to work as teammates, while providing you with the entertainment of a board game. Set up as teams to challenge your opponents with fast action game play. By matching the game cards and moving down the field, your objective is to outscore your opponents before time runs out. Each player will lay their cards on the board and try to match, when a successful match is made the board is cleared and the attempt on an offensive advance is made for a second consecutive match. The yardage cards provided will move you forward for the quick score, or move you backward for loss yardages. But watch out for the turnovers, these cards could allow your opponents to score. Watch as the anticipation mounts as each passing card chooses the fate of your team and your record

Includes playing board, 64 playing cards, 40 home/away yardage cards, two game pieces, 100 score sheets, and a pencil.

KootiKooti is an addictive, strategic interactive game that is played with 2.5” flexible rubber game pieces. Using a finger to nudge or flip their KootiKooti, 2 to 4 players take turns, using their skill and judgment to try and ‘pin down’ their opponents KootiKooti to win the round.

Series 1 comprises 50 unique designed colorful game pieces for kids to collect and trade.

This abstract strategy game plays on 9x9 black and white board. Each players control 3 peons which can be displaced in straight line according to the result of a single dice roll. The object of the game is to capture bricks located on the center or bricks which have already been collected and placed at the opponent’s side, of the board and bring them back on his own side of the board. In addition to collection bricks, one's peons can be used to directly attack his opponent’s peons, with the effect of sending back the attacked peon in on his originating camp.

From the insert:

The object of this game is to be the first player to land on each of the six blue Safe Driver Stars by exact roll of 1 or 2 dice while traveling clockwise around the gameboard. Player draws the Safe Driver Card from the deck on gameboard which matches the Safe Driver Star on which he has landed. First player to reach HOME with all six different Safe Driver Cards wins the game.

Parts list: 1 gameboard and box with rules platform and plastic tray; 14 Violation cards; 4 AAA Membership cards; 4 sets of 6 Safe Driver Cards; 2 dice; 4 playing pieces; 1 returnable market research card; and approximately $850 play money.

- - - - - - - -

The market research card (return address: Interstate Games, 35 Morrison St. Manchester, NH 03104) asks if you enjoyed playing the game, found the directions easy to follow; learned more about AAA services, etc. It also asks how many times you played it and with what ages of players (10-15, 15-20, 20-50, and over 50). Box says for ages 8-80.

Anyway, I wonder how many surveys were returned to them.

This game was available solely through the magazine "Essen & Trinken" (a magazine for cooking). 
You objective is it to get as many menus as possible. The menu always consist of 4 courses. You collect by drawing a card from a course of your choice. The card shows the kind of menu (classic, veg. etc.)and the value of that dish. You move forward as many spots as the value of the cards - depending on the place you finish you might have to answer a question or you can trade cards with other players.
At the end only finished menus are counted (and all menue cards have to be from the same kind).

All menu-cards contain a real recipe from the magazine, so you can actually use this game as a cook book (if you can understand German)

All change is a tile placement word game that uses a turntable with 4 word slots. Players must place letters into the slot in front of them to make a 3 or 4 letter word.

The turntable is then rotated to present the players with one of the other words, one that their opponents have been working on, which they must add to, change or remove tiles from to make a new word.

boardgame: The New York City Trivia Game
description:
The New York City Trivia Game is a fun way to learn about the people, places, events, geography, public transportation, museums, theaters, sports, stores, restaurants and media that make New York City so great.

NYTG will appeal to New York natives and one-time visitors alike. The City encompasses cultures and traditions the world over, so whether you play NYTG to test how much you already know or simply to learn more, you're sure to have a great time celebrating the diversity of New York City. [Excerpt from game introduction]

Move down the island of Manhattan, starting in Harlem and ending at the Statue of Liberty, answering questions in any one of four categories: Landmarks and History; Street Smarts; Arts, Entertainment and Sports; and Shopping, Eating and Media. NYTG will test and expand your knowledge about New York City. [Excerpt from game back of game box]

Help your 3 little ants find their way to their nest. Avoid the holes near the path where you can hide (get out of the game) from your enemies, which are mixed with good characters in some memory cards. So you have a good chance to stay on the right path.
A game which comes with rules in 8 languages (D,USA,F,E,P,J,NL,I)

boardgame: Screamin' Genie: The Frantic Sound & Race Game
description:
From the box:

"Somewhere far away, in a deep, dark cave, lies a magic lamp full of precious stones. The lamp is guarded by a sleeping but fearsome genie.

Now you are about to discover this magic lamp. Your goal is to capture the precious stones, but to do it, you must first rub the lamp. Do you dare risk disturbing the genie to get them?

If the genie wakes up, you will be forced into a frantic race against time to save your stones and stop the genie as he screams! Can you win the race before the screaming stops?"
This is a game where you try to get a suitable place in heaven via roll and move.  The pieces are wooden angel meeples and there are tokens you collect to put you in a particular choir placement.

"Experience this exciting card game with the entire family!"

This is an "Oh Hell!" knockoff with just a few minor changes.  The deck is a standard 52, nicely done in four bold, primary colored suits and clear numbering 1 through 13.  There are also four "Back Alley" cards that serve as ubertrump wild cards. Prior to bidding, each player may exchange one of the cards in their hand for one of the cards not dealt, drawn blindly in turn order.  The scoring is also changed.  If the bid is met, you score three points for tricks bid and taken, plus one point for tricks greater than the bid.  If the bid isn't met, it's minus one point for each trick missed.

The game comes with clear rules that also give instructions for additional players.  A nice score pad in also included.

Players are photographers trying to take the best pictures of geysers, hot springs, wildlife, hot springs, and scenery in Yellowstone National Park. Timing of geyser eruptions is card-driven. The board represents a portion of Yellowstone, and the game uses a point-to-point movement system.

The publisher's description:

There's nothing like the frenzied life of a photographer on a short deadline. You and your fellow shutterbugs have been sent to Yellowstone National Park with only one day to get all the pictures for a twelve-month calendar. At the end of the day, your publisher will pick the twelve best photos for the calendar. Your goal: get more pictures in the calendar than anyone else.

It won’t be easy. Should you gamble on the uncertain schedule of erupting geysers? Or stake out watering holes in hopes of getting a great shot of the wildlife? Grab your camera and get out on the road; you have until sundown to collect as many snapshots as you can. And don’t forget to take off your lens cap.

Geysers of Yellowstone is a quick-playing board game about the thrilling world of nature photography. Players move on a board that represents Yellowstone National Park, taking pictures of geysers, hot springs, wildlife, and scenery. Timing of geyser eruptions is card-driven. The player with the best photo portfolio at the end of the game is the winner.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: A family dinner is arranged and you are one of the members: however, this family has trust issues, and no one's certain who will spoil the pot! Dinner is served in this game of bluffing, cruelty and fun.

Baseball is great family fun. Even young children can get a base hit or bring the whole team home with a dinger. Bible Baseball has been a home run in the classroom for years. Whether you use it as a “seventh inning stretch” between lesson series or as a “sandlot” game in a classroom learning center, Bible Baseball is a Christian education MVP. What a great combination: the big league thrill of baseball and the Word of God. Cheer your friends and family on as the knock one out of the park. You’ll have a great time and will also learn about the Bible.

The pitcher has hundreds of questions about God’s Word and the right answer can get the players on first, second, or third base. They can even get a home run! The colorful newly designed game board and pieces help keep track of who is on which base as well as the score and the number of outs. All that’s missing are the hot dogs and peanuts! Bible Baseball is great for family fun, but it also is helpful in the classroom or Sunday School setting.

From front cover:

"A Beautiful Game of Skill, Strategy, and Maneuvers"

From back cover:

Maneuvering is the new easy to learn game.  It is an action packed board game which combines skill, chance, and special maneuvers to determine a winner.  It incorporates an entirely new concept which is sophisticated and considerably more attractive than conventional board games. (i.e., chess or backgammon).  The game is played on a 15 1/2" x 15 1/2" Maneuvering board where each player has twenty-four ships to protect and defend.  To start the game, these ships are positioned in the first three rows of the board (as shown).  For each turn, the player rolls two dice.  He then moves 2 ships, one for each die the number of spaces indicated by both dice.  The object of the game is to capture all of your opponent's ships or to win by trapping your opponent's ship(s).  The first player to complete one of the game objectives is the winner!
Hearts & Spades consists of 164 cards in two suits: Hearts and Spades. Score the least points by being the first player or team to exhaust your stockpile by playing on community piles.

Terran was produced in Perth, Western Australia.  
It is basically an 8 x 8 chess style board with pieces moving around on the vertices's instead of in the squares.  There is an interesting center vertex which allows the bishop type pieces to change from one set of diagonals to the other.  There are five different types of pieces all with differing moves of 1 or 2 spaces (like restricted chess pieces).  The pieces are bright yellow and red plastic.  The aim of the game is to capture the opposition command center piece.

Well researched, light Ancients rules covering the Greek and Persian Wars, the campaign of Alexander the Great, and the Second Punic War.

Written for individually based miniatures in movement trays. 
Each unit is rated for shooting, melee, morale and special abilities, and move and fight in strict formation. Fighting involves rolling lots of dice.

The rule set includes army lists and historical scenarios.

An early game from the designer of Hey, That's My Fish!

The game is played on the intersections of a 9×9 grid, each player receives 34 pieces in their color.

A player's turn consists of either
1) Moving 1 piece to a neighboring empty point (optional), followed by placing a new piece on any empty point. (required)

or

2) Taking pieces by jumping over horizontal or vertical lines of their opponent's pieces. If this is possible, this must be done instead of moving/placing pieces via option 1.

Lines with 2 pieces cannot be jumped over, but any other amount can be. The jumping piece must continue jumping until no more jumps are possible. After each jump, the opponent's pieces are captured and removed from the board. If you capture the same number of pieces that your opponent just captured from you on the preceding turn, the pieces are returned to the opponent and not placed in your captured pile.

The game ends when a player cannot make any legal move. Each player gets one point for each captured opponent's piece, plus one point for each opponent's piece on the outside border of the board. Most points wins.

Let's Go Hike is a simple 'spin & move' game set in the Boundary Waters Canoe Area.  Players select from an assortment of forest creature markers such as 'Shirley Squirrel' or 'Matthew Mallard', and race from the Gunflint Trail Ranger Station to the goal: Moon Lake campsite.

The board depicts stars connected to each other by lines.  Each player takes turn placing a marker on a star, scoring one point for each connected star containing an opposing-player marker.  The game ends when the board is filled.  The player with the most points win.

Galaxy was created for Chief Herman's Holiday Fun Pack.

An interesting game that verges on being just a toy.  There is a plastic muscle-man, with an empty barbell, and yellow plastic discs as weights.  The game employs a winch system and a turn-crank, by which the players move the Mr. Muscle figure's arms to lift the barbell.

Theme: Weightlifting.  Children's game.

The Napoleonic Wars covers the entire period of conflict from 1796 to 1815.  Each turn represents 4 months.  The map is divided into areas, with terrain in the area (forest, swamp, etc.) and along the area borders (river, mountains, etc.). According to the rules, the pieces represent small corps or oversized divisions.  Some units also represent commanders that can command other units.  Fortresses are also represented by pieces.

The sequence of play is fairly standard for a wargame - 
1) Negotiations 
2) Rebuilding Cadres
3) Supply Determination
4) Movement and Combat
5) Reinforcements 
6) Adjustment of Revenue 
7) Move game turn marker

When playing one of the campaigns (i.e., not the full Napoleonic Wars), skip Negotiations, Rebuilding, and Revenue.

The Movement phase has a unique mechanic that requires units with lower (worse) leadership to move before those with better leadership, regardless of which country controls the unit.  Units which don't move when it is their turn may only move later if led by a commander with higher leadership.  A player may use his highest rated leader to preempt the movement of another player's lower rated leader.

This game was sold as a stand alone game with a paper map.  The game could also be played by combining the pieces and mounted maps from the other three games in the series (Napoleon's Italian Campaigns, Napoleon's Russian Campaign, and Napoleon's Peninsula Campaign) and adding the extra rules and pieces from Expansion Module 2.
This is a game that lets a person learn about the musical instruments of the West.  It has five different games for various age ranges, ranging from 4 years to adult.

boardgame: Murder à la carte: A Cajun Killing
description:
On the box:

The mystery - The scene is a riverboat plying the Mississippi.  Among the passengers is Benton Thorne, a plantation owner infamous for his ruthlessness.  The vacation his wife Rosie persuaded him to take is brought to an abrupt end by a murderer's blade.  With so many enemies, that's no surprise...or is it?

Sylabex is a word-making game, easy to play in which both skill and luck are involved.

The cards all bear a three-letter syllable and a number (which is the points value used in scoring). Any cards when placed beside certain of the other cards forms a complete six letter word, or in some cases will form a nine letter word.

The object of the game is to make words with all the cards in your hand. Other players score the points value of the cards in hand when the round ends, and the winner is the player with the lowest score after the agreed numbers of rounds.

boardgame: PBI II: 15mm Scale Company Actions of World War 2
description:
From publisher:
PBI is a game of company level combat in WW2. PBI is an infantry game with armor present as support. Artillery and the like is off table.

A set of WW2 company level rules. The game is played on a 4 foot by 4 foot grid, split into 6" squares. A company would typically consist of 3 tanks and 25 bases of infantry.

The scale is 1 to 1. This means that a base of three figures represents three men. A tank model represents a real tank. The tabletop scale is 10m to the inch.

The Overall concept.

PBI is intended to provide a two hour wargame. All of the dice used are simple D6. All of the mechanisms are intuitive. PBI uses a gridded board. This means that all movement, range and areas are described in terms of squares. Because the player takes the role of the company commander, the squares of the grid allow him to get soldiers to "go over there and do that", rather than measuring distances and engaging in single figure management. By using a grid it is very clear what scenery type the figures are in. There are three types of square. Closed (buildings and woods), Partial (hedges, walls and fences) and open (no significant cover).

The rules generate a scenario which both players have a fair chance of winning. The game is always of the attack/defense style.

PBI uses a lot of D6 (a typical section shooting would roll 10 dice). This helps give a feel for the massive amount of ammunition expended and allows the probabilities to even out (the more dice you roll, the more predictable the result).

The aim of PBI is to create company level engagements which players can win by use of better tactics. The game is focussed on the player being a company commander so those are the tasks given to the player.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players
Series: Poor Bloody Infantry – WWII Miniature Rules (Peter Pig)
Series: Rule For The Common Man (Peter Pig)

description: An exciting game of Travel & Trivia using a factual world map as your playing board.  Players start with airline itineraries & travelers checks and need to be the first player to visit all the cities on their itinerary, collecting the appropriate visa, and return to their country of origin all the while managing their money, navigating detours & diversions.  The first player to collect 10 visas and return to their country of origin is declared the winner.  PLAY:  Players must announce their intended destination before the role of the die which will determine the amount of the fare.   The die role will determine the destination and the cost by displaying full/half or free fares.  If a player rolls divert, transit, geography or general knowledge then they must take the appropriate card from the board and their travel is suspended or diverted depending on the instructions of the card.  Many times players may answer trivia questions about their particular region to allow their flight to continue to their previously stated destination, however, a wrong answer usually keeps the player grounded or relocates them to an undesirable location.   Money is collected by opting not to fly and answering trivia questions or a combination of both.  The Australian game designer recommends this game for ages 12+.

Players start with 5 numbered tokens of their color. They must get rid of these, and acquire 5 new ones not of their color. Every spot on the board has a token/number associated with it. The board is a hexagon linked with 6 paths through the center. The players move counter-clockwise or through the center.

The Frontline General 'Land, Air, & Sea Tabletop Simulation' is an Operational and Tactical Level WWII Consim.  The system covers Land, Air, and Sea wargaming mostly at the Operational level with a tactical 'feel'.  The board wargame may be used to 'drive' more detailed tabletop miniatures battles for decisive engagements.  Miniatures combat expands on the level of detail and is entirely optional.

Frontline General: Italian Campaign expands on the published introductory game, Frontline General: Italian Campaign Introduction, by adding a large size map of Southern Italy (See the Image Gallery), more Units, an additional country (the United Kingdom), and five historical scenarios covering action from Salerno to the Gustav Line.

The game will be available as a standalone game or as an upgrade to the Frontline General: Italian Campaign Introduction.

The introductory version linked above is the first release in the series and is currently available as a production version and a free Print & Play version.

Collins Epic Wargames provides rules support via website forums and e-mail and also publishes a Newsletter called Frontline General News with in-depth articles, examples, and previews of upcoming products.  All back issues of FG News are available online.

From the box back:

Kids love playgrounds - an they love counting, too!  In this clever preschool counting game, players travel around the colorful paths in search of LEGO bricks.  Each LEGO brings players one step closer to reaching the tree house - winning the game!

Along the way, they may get to play hopscotch, which gives them an extra turn.  Look out for the sandbox - land in it, and you'll lose a turn!

Three levels of play make this a game for even the youngest players.

Renaissance Chess (also known as Rennchess) is a large board (10 ranks x 12 files) chess variant with each player fielding an army of 34 pieces.  The 16 pieces found in an orthodox chess set are present along with some very powerful new pieces such as the Prince (combines the movement of a Queen and Knight), the Nobleman (combines the movement of the Rook and Knight), the Archbishop (combines the movement of the Bishop and Knight), the Cavalier (a side-stepping Rook type), the Duke (a side-stepping Bishop type), the Page (combines the movement of a King and Knight), and the Squire (moves or leaps 1 or 2 squares in a straight line).

The castling rules of orthodox chess are abandoned, but pawns are still able to utilize en-passant capture and retain their two-step first move capability.  Pawns which reach the opponent's back rank must promote to a previously captured piece.  Foxes which reach the opponent's back rank may optionally promote to a Guard.

According to Eric Greenwood, the game's author (in an article on ChessVariants.org), the correct spelling of the game should be "Renniassance" (the first "n" doubled and the "ai" reversed).

boardgame: Herocard Champion of New Olympia Ferrion Expansion Deck
description:
From the back of the box:

FERRION EXPANSION DECK
Ferrion crushes evil with techno armor, advanced weapons, and maverick piloting skills.

Ferrion is a HeroCard Expansion Deck for HeroCard Champion of New Olympia, the ultimate fight for glory. Ferrion can compete in any HeroCard game or be combined with other decks to create a customized character.

CONTENTS
33 Character Cards
1 Figure

Expands

    HeroCard Champion of New Olympia

A game about horse racing in the American Kentucky Derby / Australian Melbourne Cup.  This is the re-themed,revamped and updated Whitman game that was first put out in 1938 as "Kentucky Derby". This is the revamped and updated Whitman game that was first put out in 1938. The 1961 version features much a fancier board with nicely rendered horses, betting slips and scorecards.

Re-implements:

    Kentucky Derby

This is a race between 2-4 gangsters and up to 23 policemen. The board depicts a town with 170 hex spaces. 60 of them are specially marked and are filled with turned down "houses".

1 player takes the role of the police and the others take the role of the gangsters. The gangsters can enter the houses and take the tiles laying there. Some of them are worth just money, but there are also tiles with several transportation possibilities like cars or planes, which allows faster movement than normal or robbing a bank.

While the game proceeds, the police player will get more and more policemen. He must try to eliminate the gangsters by surrounding them completely. The game ends when all gangsters have left the town.

A little strange the victory conditions: the gangster with the highest amount of prey is the winner. There is no given victory condition for the police player, but it's suggested to play as many games as there are players, so each player has been the police once!

boardgame: Der Weg nach Drakonia Essen '05 Promo Cards
description: Four additional cards for "Der Weg nach Drakonia". First a give-away at Essen 2005, now (as long as supplies last) available by mail from Fanpro.

boardgame: The Art of War: Tactical Warfare in Miniature for Pre-gunpowder Armies
description:
Short version of the game is: It plays fast. Usually a battle takes about an hour. It doesn't really matter how the figs are based. Its fairly bloody. Figs are pulled as they are killed. It has the tactics mechanisms built in, so you really only need to worry about what the army you're running is capable of. We strive for historical accuracy, and have a pretty high success rate for duplicating real fights.

* Basic rules, plus a Crusades mini-campaign appendix including 9 armies lists: 62 pages. The Basic Rules are comprehensive, and include siege warfare rules and rules for special weapons such as elephants, chariots and pavises (a large convex shield of European origin used to protect the entire body).
    * Standard 15mm to 40mm frontages used: no rebasing of figurines required. (Although singly-based miniatures work best, multiple-figurine bases work OK in most situations.)
    * Plus 84 additional pages of seven period appendices listing over 160 more armies:
    * Greek & Macedonian Armies
    * Ancient Armies of the Bronze Age
    * Ancient Armies of the Iron Age
    * Ancient Roman and Italian Armies
    * European Dark Ages Armies 500 to 1000 A. D.
    * European Medieval Armies 1000 to 1460 A. D.
    * Chinese, Huns, Mongols and Japanese Armies

Download version can be purchased at www.wargamedownloads.com

boardgame: Princess Ryan's Space Marines: Squad Combat in the 23rd Century
description:
Princess Ryan's Space Marines is a simple game of individual and small unit combat in the not too distant future. Tested at many conventions by gamers of all ages, it is easy enough for grammar school and junior high students to play, yet is challenging enough for the veteran gamer. Although set in its own speculative history, the system is very flexible. It fits in well with almost any other science fiction setting or universe, including those you create yourself.

includes:
- 48 lead armor units @ 1/285 scale
- 2 dice
- instructions & scenarios(3) booklet
- Combat Results Table (CRT)

Re-implemented by:

    Princess Ryan's Star Marines

boardgame: HeroCard Champion of New Olympia: Talon Expansion Deck
description:
From the back of the box:

TALON EXPANSION DECK
Talon hunts his prey using his uncanny instincts and his deadly arsenal.

Talon is a HeroCard Expansion Deck for HeroCard Champion of New Olympia, the ultimate fight for glory. Talon can compete in any HeroCard game or be combined with other decks to create a customized character.

CONTENTS
33 Character Cards
1 Figure

Expands

    HeroCard Champion of New Olympia

"You are about to experience the Hawaiian Scene -- living and working in a Pacific paradise while purchasing and developing your favorite Island properties.  A weekend in Maui, snorkeling at Hanauma Bay, and the excitement of Waikiki -- all part of the Hawaiian Scene.  Before you play be prepared to deal with the "Big Five," make peace with labor leaders, influence the Mayor and hire a Kahuna to ensure your success.  Get ready to move fast, play hard, and take risks -- for you're in Hawaii and rolling for high stakes!"
Basically this is a travel variant of Monopoly, with some added mechanics in the form of collecting labor leaders and Kahunas to increase your capital and leverage.  The properties are all Hawaiian businesses circa 1978.

From the website:

Big Sunday Football plays closer to real football than any other game. Running, Passing, Kicking, Penalties, Sacks, Interceptions, Fumbles, 2-minute Warning, 2 point conversion, bad call by officials flag picked up. You name it, Big Sunday has it, except audibles. For ages 7-adult. Endorsed by NFL Coaches and Players. When you roll doubles look to the rule chart.
You may have broken a tackle or if you sacked the QB doubles means he fumbled the ball. You may have intercepted the pass and three doubles in a row means Touchdown return.

boardgame: Man o' War: Napoleonic Naval Warfare Rules
description:
A very concise rule set for Napoleonic naval warfare using 1/3000 scale ship miniatures. The rules are self described as having "loopholes in them, but if you like your games fast, furious and bloody, these rules should work for you".
There is much definition missing from the rules, and they would appeal to gaming friends who do not mind compromising on complexity, and who are not prone too much to arguing over rules.
Damage is recorded on record cards.

boardgame: Sword and Pistol: Rules for Renaissance Skirmishes
description:
Rules for Renaissance Skirmishes

The English Civil War, the Three Musketeers, Swords and the fall of the Armored Knight are all included within this skirmish game.

To be fought between 8-12 figures on a side, the detail is complete to replicate the period fighting style(s) and technology.

28 pages and a Quick Reference sheet that pulls out of the booklet.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: This is a fast reaction game where you try be the last one with all your marbles in your upper ramp zone.  A motorized skunk runs around a track and will bump your character if you do not push a button down fast enough to have your character jump out of his way.  If he bumps you, the marble will roll down to the lower zone; once all your marbles are at the bottom, you are out.  Last one with marbles in the top zone wins.

boardgame: Battle-Axe: Wargames Rules for Medieval Skirmishes
description:
Wargame for Medieval Skirmishes

A painted knight on a hex base take on a man at arms while a couple of archers fling arrows at him. How he swings his sword is factored in the turn and the priority of thrusting before the cut or swings will dictates who will win.

28 pages with some hex pattern "board", charts for orders and abilities and a pull out Summary Sheet help the gameplay.

One small Erratum sheet:

Please note that on the summary sheet 2 for firing and melee, the Melee Procedure contains a typing error:
Line 4 should read "For each hit throw 3 D6 & add attacker's AA."

This is a game based on the TV show Winx Club and is meant for ages 6 and up.

The aim of the game is to be the first to finish the complete path of the board. This board contains 2 parts, one round for each of the 3 Colleges and an external round, common for all players.

Each player gets 5 cards from the stack with "Magic"- cards, 2 cards from the stack with Special Cards. The cards should be kept hidden from the other players.

Each player rolls the 6 sided die and moves his pawn accordingly on the board. After finishing the inner College round, the route goes further around the outer board. The first player to reach the circle numbered 66 is the winner of the game.

On several places on the board there are things happening which can influence the course of the game. Some duals have to be solved with the aid of "Special"-cards, or questions have to be answered from the stack with "Fairy, Witch or Specialist"-cards.

The "Magical"-cards  represent  the ingredients needed for the spells.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Retinue

Per Wikipedia:
A retinue (Old French retenue, from retenir also meaning 'to employ', from Latin retenere, hold back, retain) is a body of persons "retained" in the service of a noble or royal personage, a suite (literal French meanings: what follows) of "retainers."

A Retinue is sometimes confused with an entourage, which is the far less stable body of people that followed whether or not they were -or claimed to be- retained or protected by the prominent person they served.

This game, albiet about knights fighting knights, recreates the true method of travel for a knight during the Middle Ages; Never alone!

This skirmish rule set of 42 pages and 2 pull out charts moves from a simple Retinue moving and meeting a group of bandits to Siege warfare. Suitable for a campaign game and weekend warriors, and provides some historical insight on the middle ages (A Knight's Tale" not withstanding)

This game is a variant of Monopoly, with cards and property names changed to items of interest for Boston University students, alumni and staff.  Although the basic gameplay is the same as Monopoly, the dollar values and card text has changed in B.U.opoly, resulting in different economics than the game upon which it is based.
publishers: Late for the Sky Production Company

description: An old German game, published in the 40s. An easy one with fighters, bombers, recons, Flak and balloons.The attacker has to fight for the railway station and the defender has to defend it.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Wise Guys is a collection of 10 spelling games that utilize the same board and letter tiles.  The letters are divided into three groups: Blue tiles (vowels-A,E,I,O), Green tiles (common consonants & "U"), and Pink tiles (less common consonants & wild "infinity" tiles).  Certain spaces on the board have a color & symbol that might affect scoring or word placement.

Some of the games are simple and suitable for young spellers, while other games are more complex.  The winning condition for most games is either (1) to score more points than opponents or (2) to use more letter tiles than opponents.  Players face their own letters in the upright position based on their seat at the table, and, in most games, your letters cannot contact opponents' words.  When scoring is based on points, the letter value is based on its color -- one point for blue, two for green and three for pink.

"An exciting adventure game"

You're sailing the South Seas with Pippi and the pirates, on an exciting cruise on Pippi's ship Hoppetossa. While visiting a small island, Pippi's feet start to tingle strangely - it's time for a good old brawl and fight after a long sailing! Challenge the five pirates - Pollus, Hot Tin Pan, Oomalat, Dummerkopfen and cook O'Malley - into a dice duel on the gangplank. You'll also need gold coins from the island to bribe them. If you win the pirate you'll get to push him into the sea - but if you lose, it's YOU who'll end up in the drink, and need to swim back to the island for some more coins from Pippi's famous suitcase! Once you get to the ship itself, you need to face ALL the remaining pirates one after the other - unless you're lucky and they're all swimming already, in which case you win an instant victory and get to hoist the flag in Hoppetossa's mast!

(From the Introduction in the rules.)
Regimental Chess is ...  a battle by a division of chess pieces on a (board) three times larger than traditional chess.

Each side has 14 infantrymen (pawns), 4 bishops, 4 knights, 4 rooks, 2 queens, and a king. Pieces may move individually or in battalions. A battalion is the group of each piece type, e.g. 4 bishops are a battalion. Battalion movement is all pieces making the same move (e.g., three diagonally northwest). It's like station keeping in naval fleet movement. Or battalions can move in a chain, each advancing forward to take the place of the other, like marching forward. All pieces have to spaced apart equally for chain movement.

It was a commercially available game. The map provided was a roll up chess board. The pieces were of good quality plastic in the traditional Staunton chess piece style.

A free to print game by Patrice Vernet with the same mechanics of Katmandou.

The game is set in medieval Europe, when most of it was suffering from Plague. Your mission is to eradicate the plague, by controlling  population movements and by using the expertise of special characters (Inquisitor, Physician, Wizard, etc.).

This game is a more spiced up version of Katmandou.

The center of the huge board shows a scene from Robinson Crusoe, on the outer part of the board are 4 images in 4 different colors. Each player plays with the 4 images of his color, that is he collects multi-colored tiles, which match the drawings on the images.

Tiles are won through the throw of 2-4 dice, depending on the number of "finished" images.

Winner is the player who completes the 4 images first.

Schmetterling (=butterfly) contains 44 flower tiles, 20 butterfly tiles and 4 tokens for each player. The flower tiles are diagonally separated into 2 halves showing two parts of a flower. Both halves have also a number on it (0-4). The butterfly tiles have numbers from 4 - 24.

Tiles are laid on the table like in dominoes: flower parts must have the same colors are adjacent flower parts. Every time, when a player lays the 4th flower part, a whole flower is completed and that player gets a butterfly card and puts a token on the flower to mark it as his flower.

When the first player has put all his 4 tokens on the table, the tile laying phase ends and the final calculation is done. The numbers on all 4 parts of completed flowers are summarized and if the player possessing it has a butterfly tile with that number, he scores these points. Winner is the player with the most points.

The game is played of a board which reminds of the petals of a flower. Each player has 1 moving token only, but there are also 70 neutral (blocking) tokens in the game. The idea of Blockade predates Amazons.

At the beginning, both player place their moving and one neutral token on the board. Each subsequent turn, a player must move his token to an adjacent empty space. Then, he places a neutral token on the board trying to surround his opponent. The players wins who manages that the token of his opponent cannot move anymore.

On the first move, a restriction applies for both players. Neither the moving nor the blocking token may be placed in a marked area in the center of the board. Together with the slightly irregular board, this is supposed to cancel out any first move advantage.

Each player has a set of 20 tiles of different shapes and sizes: 12 x 5-size, 5 x 4-size, 2 x 3-size and 1 x 2-size (88 squares in total). The game is played on a 14x14 board, but five 2x2 spaces are forbidden areas - giving 176 playable spaces.

Each tile has small triangles on all sides. If 2 pieces are laying attached to each other, the triangles form squares on the common sides. These squares are called "towers" and they are important to capture opposing pieces, since they represent the strength of attacking/defending tiles.

Winner of the game is the player who is the first who can lay down all his 20 tiles on the board.

Wind up the time bomb and the players toss the loudly ticking device amongst themselves. The player caught holding the device when it stops ticking and pops is eliminated from play. The sole surviving player is the winner.

This is nothing more than a book outlining 14 outdoor activities (including several games) that can be played with the included sidewalk chalk (in 6 colors, hence the suggested maximum number of players in this entry).

Some games are no-brainers (Hopscotch, Tic-Tac-Toe) others are a bit unusual (drawing a race track and alternately flicking chalk to draw lines of advancement around the track).

Essentially, it's a structured set of sidewalk chalk.

This very early game from McLoughlin Brothers of New York was produced just as automobiles came on to the scene as viable transportation. The map showed the U.S., southern Canada, and northern Mexico with major cities such as New York, New Orleans, Mexico City, Los Angeles and Quebec depicted.

In this game, players race across the country. The cars are moved by spinning a cardboard rondel.

The game was a simple spin and move game which featured spots on the board where one might lose a turn because of breakdowns or repairs.

Tanbark Trail attempts to simulate the exciting world of Holstein show ring competition. The object of the game is to win ribbons and trophies which at the end of the game are converted to points. The player with the most points wins.

At the start of the game, a set number of cattle are auctioned off to the players. During the game, players may purchase more cattle from the sales barn, sell animals back to the sales barn, or trade or purchase from each other.

There are also random event cards drawn during the game that operate much like "Chance" or "Community Chest" cards in Monopoly.

Players move around a board depicting a fairgrounds, at the center of which is the show ring. Any time a player moves his token into the show ring, a competition begins. At this point players must pay an entry fee for each animal they wish to enter in each show class. First, second, and third-place winners are determined by combining die rolls with stats on that animal's card.

The first three rounds of show competition are considered to be State competitions, and the fourth a National competition. After the fourth round, all award ribbons are converted to points, and the winner is determined.

'Starsiege: Rebellion' is a miniatures game set in Sierra/Dynamix's "Starsiege" universe.  Forces present include the Imperials (humans from Earth), Rebels (human colonials on Mars who stumble into advanced alien technology to allow them to rebel), and Cybrids (humanity's long-time enemy...machines who attempted to destroy their creators several times in the past, and each time being beaten back).  At the point of the game, the Cybrids had established a final base around Pluto from which they began a new invasion after the humans started fighting amongst themselves.

There are not many 'scenarios', but the production values are pretty decent - and the game box includes a bucket of miniatures.

The gameplay features unit activation as a combination of laying chits by units and rolling a die, and the combat has some very elegant twists that make it resolve FAST - only 2 dice (one determined by range, the other determined by skill of the firing unit) for are needed to resolve all damage from an attack.  As a result, the game is not very "nitty gritty", like Battletech, but takes a higher-level view of action.  Scenarios played with a dozen units would be over in half an hour, easily, and scenarios with many dozens of units would be playable in an afternoon.

Boxed set includes:

     96-page rulebook 
     29 vehicle data cards
     Master Equipment/Master Weapons Reference Card
     Meeting Engagement Scenario Setup Card
     FAQ (Frequently Asked Questions) Sheet
     80 full-color die-cut counters
     5 full-color die-cut templates
     5 dice (4-, 6-, 8-, 10-, and 12-sided) 
     18 unpainted miniatures


Imperial
- 4 Talon Scout Hercs 
- 4 Obsidian Hover Tanks 
- 1 Minotaur Herc 
Cybrid 
- 4 Seeker Scout Hercs 
- 4 Manta Hover Tanks 
- 1 Shepherd Herc 
- 4 hills (thermal plastic, flocked, from 2" to 7" across)

Buzzword -- The Fastest Word Game Ever!

Players roll out their letter dice, then take turns making words from the upturned letters.  The first player places the first word on a "start square" on the 13x13 board.  All subsequent words must use a letter from a word already on the board, or must extend or alter a word already on the board.
